attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old woman attends the surgical clinic to discuss the management plan for a recently diagnosed 2.5 cm carcinoma of her left breast via a core biopsy. Further work up of the biopsy demonstrated tumour receptor status of oestrogen receptor negative, progesterone receptor negative and HER-2 receptor positive. Staging investigations did not show evidence of distant metastases. She has a 1 cm immobile lymph node in her left axilla, which shows malignant cells on fine needle aspiration cytology. She is otherwise in good health and is postmenopausal.

During the consult she expresses her apprehension and anxiety at the thought of losing her breast as part of treatment, but also wants the best course of treatment for survival.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy
attributes.explanation:
The first step in this question is to determine what stage of cancer the patient has from the information provided: T = T2. N = N1, M = 0. This gives her stage 2B breast cancer.

The next step is to consider her receptor status as some of the options are relevant to this information. She is both oestrogen and progesterone receptor negative (ER/PR negative) therefore she is not a candidate for either letrozole or tamoxifen. Should she have been positive for these receptors then an aromatase inhibitor such as letrozole would be indicated as an adjuvant therapy, as she is postmenopausal. Tamoxifen is indicated in those with ER/PR positive breast cancer who are pre-menopausal. Neither of these would be given as a first treatment in this scenario.

Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is indicated in patients with locally advanced breast cancer or in those with earlier stage triple negative (ER/PR and HER2) or HER2-positive breast cancer. HER2-positive breast cancers are quite aggressive and have a higher sensitivity to chemotherapy and biologic agents (trastuzumab). Neoadjuvant chemotherapy / trastuzumab can aid tumour shrinkage prior to surgery thus reducing the volume of tissue to be resected. 

If the patient elects to have surgery first and not neoadjuvant therapy, then an axillary lymph node dissection would be recommended. If they elect to have neoadjuvant systemic therapy, then there is the potential to 'downstage' the axilla and perform a sentinel node biopsy and targeted axillary dissection SNB /TAD should the patient show a good response to therapy.

Wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy would not be the initial treatment of choice in this case because neoadjuvant therapy can potentially reduce the tumour size and downstage the axillary lymph nodes, allowing for less extensive surgery.

Mastectomy and axillary clearance is a more invasive surgical option that may be considered after neoadjuvant therapy, especially if the tumour does not respond well to initial treatment. However, starting with neoadjuvant therapy can help preserve breast tissue and reduce surgical morbidity.

Radiotherapy is typically used as an adjuvant treatment following surgery to reduce the risk of local recurrence. It is not used as the initial treatment in this scenario.
learningPoints: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy with anti-HER2 therapy is indicated for locally advanced or early stage HER2-positive breast cancer.
HER2-positive breast cancers are aggressive and respond well to chemotherapy and targeted therapy, which can reduce tumour size before surgery.
Neoadjuvant therapy can potentially downstage axillary lymph nodes, allowing for less extensive surgical procedures.
references: Sikov WM, Boughey JC and Al-Hilli A. General principles of neoadjuvant management of breast cancer [Internet].Uptodate; 2021 [Updated 2021' cited 2021 Sep 17]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/general-principles-of-neoadjuvant-management-of-breast-cancer#H4074438079>.

Pritchard KI. Adjuvant endocrine therapy for postmenopausal women with hormone receptor-positive breast cancer [Internet]. Uptodate; 2021 [Updated 2021; cited 2021 Sep 17]. Available from: <https://www-uptodate-com.ezproxy.flinders.edu.au/contents/adjuvant-endocrine-therapy-for-postmenopausal-women-with-hormone-receptor-positive-breast-cancer?search=ER%2FPR%20positive&source=search_result&selectedTitle=6~150&usage_type=default&display_rank=6>.

Sikov WM. Neoadjuvant therapy for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer [Internet]. Uptodate; 2021 [Updated 2021; cited 2021 Sep 17]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/neoadjuvant-therapy-for-patients-with-her2-positive-breast-cancer?topicRef=14225&source=see_link#H3102275113>.

Zaborowski AM, Wong SM. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy for breast cancer. Br J Surg. 2023 Jun 12;110(7):765-772. doi: 10.1093/bjs/znad103. PMID: 37104057; PMCID: PMC10683941. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10683941/
